response:
the environmental scientist is studying the population dynamics of an endangered species of turtle on a remote island. the turtle population is modeled by a logistic growth model with the following differential equation: $ \frac{dP}{dt} = rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) $, where $ P(t) $ is the population of turtles at time $ t $, $ r $ is the intrinsic growth rate of the population, $ K $ is the carrying capacity of the environment, and $ h(t) $ is a harvesting function representing the number of turtles lost due to environmental threats each year.

1. given that the intrinsic growth rate $ r = 0.1 $ per year, the carrying capacity $ K = 1000 $ turtles, and the harvesting function is modeled by $ h(t) = 10 + 5\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) $, determine the equilibrium population size $ P_e $ of the turtles where the population remains constant over time.

the equilibrium population size $ P_e $ is found by setting $ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0 $, which gives $ rP\left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) - h(t) = 0 $. solving for $ P $, we get $ P = 0 $ or $ P = K $. since $ P = 0 $ is trivial, the non-trivial equilibrium is $ P = K $. substituting the given values, the equilibrium population size is $ P_e = 1000 $.

2. considering the effects of climate change, the scientist predicts that the carrying capacity will decrease linearly over time according to the function $ K(t) = 1000 - 3t $, where $ t $ is measured in years. analyze the stability of the equilibrium population size $ P_e(t) $ under this new model and determine the time $ t $ when the population will no longer be sustainable (i.e., $ P_e(t) \leq 0 $). include a palindrome in your response.

the new model changes the carrying capacity over time, so the equilibrium population size $ P_e(t) $ is no longer constant. the stability of the equilibrium is determined by the derivative of the population function. the time when the population will no longer be sustainable is when $ P_e(t) \leq 0 $, which occurs at $ t =
